title: Distinguish Needs vs Wants
summary: Tell the difference between what you need and what you just want.
learnerPromise: Tell the difference between what you need and what you just want.
whyItMatters: Every dollar you spend on a want is a dollar that could have gone to a need or a savings goal. Knowing the difference gives you more power over your money.
realLifeUses: Decide whether to buy new shoes because your old ones broke or because you like the style.
Choose between eating out and cooking at home when money is tight.
Explain to a friend why you are skipping an expensive outing this month.
Prioritize bills and groceries over impulse purchases.
youWillLearn: Define a need: something required for basic living or safety.
Define a want: something nice to have but not essential.
Apply the 48-hour rule to impulse purchases.
Review your spending and label each item as need or want.
miniChallenge: Look at your last 5 purchases and label each as need or want.
steps: Write down the last 5 things you spent money on.
Ask yourself for each one: 'Could I live safely and reasonably without this?'
If yes, it is a want. If no, it is a need. Label each one.
Count how many are wants. Notice the total amount.
Pick one want you could skip next time and redirect that money toward a goal.
completionCriteria: You can look at a purchase and label it a need or a want.
You have identified at least one want you can reduce.
commonProblems: You justify wants as needs — be honest with yourself.
You feel deprived calling something a want — remember, wants are fine in moderation.
Some items are both — when in doubt, ask if there is a cheaper alternative that meets the need.
tips: The 48-hour rule: wait two days before a non-essential purchase. The urge often passes.
A need keeps you safe, healthy, or employed. Everything else is negotiable.
Frame it as a choice, not a sacrifice: 'I am choosing to save for X instead.'
